title: Pulse Oximetry
subtitle: The nurse measures SpO2 accurately — choosing a warm, well-perfused site, recognizing what lowers accuracy (poor perfusion, motion, dark polish), knowing CO poisoning reads falsely high, and always correlating with the patient.

patient.name: Pulse Oximetry
patient.admitDate: The nurse measures and interprets oxygen saturation accurately.

nursesNote: The nurse uses pulse oximetry to measure oxygen saturation, where a normal value is about 95-100%. An accurate reading requires a warm, well-perfused site (finger, toe, earlobe, or forehead) and a good pulse waveform; poor perfusion from a cold extremity, hypotension, or peripheral vascular disease, along with motion, shivering, dark nail polish, artificial nails, and bright ambient light, all reduce accuracy. The nurse knows that carbon monoxide poisoning can produce a falsely HIGH reading because the device cannot distinguish carboxyhemoglobin from oxygenated hemoglobin, and that severe anemia may read a normal percent despite low total oxygen content. The probe site is rotated to prevent skin breakdown. Above all, the nurse correlates the number with the patient's clinical status — color, work of breathing, and level of consciousness — rather than trusting the value alone, and checks the probe before acting on a low reading.
providerOrders: Monitor SpO2 using a warm, well-perfused site
Remove dark nail polish or use an alternate site if needed
Confirm a good waveform/signal before trusting the value
Correlate the reading with the patient's clinical status
Check probe placement before acting on a low reading

steps.title: Recognize Cues
steps.sub: What is true about pulse oximetry?

opts.text: A normal oxygen saturation is about 95-100%
Poor perfusion or a cold extremity can cause an inaccurate reading
Dark nail polish can interfere with the reading
The number is more important than the patient's appearance
Motion and shivering can cause artifact
The probe site should be rotated to prevent skin injury
Carbon monoxide poisoning can give a falsely high reading
A good pulse waveform indicates a reliable signal
Bright ambient light can interfere with the reading
Pulse oximetry alone rules out all oxygenation problems
Severe anemia can read a normal percent despite low oxygen content
Warming a cold hand can improve the reading

steps.title: Prioritize Hypotheses
steps.sub: Order obtaining an accurate SpO2 reading

opts.text: Select a warm, well-perfused site
Remove nail polish or use an alternate site if needed
Apply the probe and check for a good waveform/signal
Read the SpO2 once the signal is stable
Correlate the reading with the patient's clinical status
Warming a cold hand before placing the probe
Dark nail polish on the probe finger
Ensuring a good pulse waveform/signal
The patient shivering or moving the hand
Choosing a well-perfused site
A bright procedure light shining on the probe
Removing artificial nails or using another site
Very low blood pressure with poor perfusion

steps.title: Take Action
steps.inst: Rank the order of actions. #1 = FIRST.

opts.text: Check the probe placement and signal
Assess the patient (breathing, color, LOC)
Reposition the patient and encourage deep breaths
Apply oxygen as ordered or indicated
Notify the provider if there is no improvement
Choosing a warm, well-perfused site for the probe
Trusting an SpO2 of 99% in a carbon-monoxide victim
Correlating the number with the patient's appearance
Ignoring a low reading without checking the probe
Removing dark nail polish before measuring
Leaving the probe on one finger for days
Confirming a good waveform before reading
Treating the number while the patient looks cyanotic and struggling

reference: Aligned to the NCSBN NCLEX Test Plan client-need category: Reduction of Risk Potential, using the NCSBN Clinical Judgment Measurement Model. Written from standard nursing references. Pending faculty review.
